serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish serving of bok choy in oyster sauce is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a small plate of 3–4 stalks of blanched or stir-fried bok choy generously coated in oyster sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pak Choi (Bok Choy), cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 170390) and Oyster Sauce (FDC ID 172237), SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked bok choy (FDC ID 170390) and oyster sauce (FDC ID 172237), combined in proportions consistent with a standard Vietnamese bok choy in oyster sauce preparation (approximately 80% bok choy, 20% oyster sauce mixture including oil). The input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA component data and retained where consistent. Energy (60 kcal/100g), protein (2.25 g), fat (3.0 g), and carbohydrate (6.0 g) are plausible for this preparation and consistent with USDA component data. Sodium (475 mg/100g) is consistent with oyster sauce contribution and aligns with USDA oyster sauce data. No exact composite dish entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore for this specific Vietnamese preparation; values are therefore classified as adapted estimates from approved USDA SR Legacy component data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021;114(5):1625–1632.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bok choy in oyster sauce as a composite dish. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked non-starchy cruciferous green vegetables (e.g., cooked cabbage GI ~10, cooked broccoli GI ~10–15) reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed international GI tables published in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. Oyster sauce contributes a small amount of sugar and cornstarch but the overall carbohydrate load remains low (6 g/100g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 12.0) / 100 = 1.8 for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 475 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold due to oyster sauce and soy sauce content
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish as prepared; if consumed, request sauce on the side or ask for low-sodium soy sauce and reduced oyster sauce to bring sodium below 200 mg/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6 g/100g), low GI (~15), and dietary fibre (1.5 g/100g) from bok choy moderate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in standard portions; monitor sauce quantity as oyster sauce contains added sugar, and request lighter sauce application to minimise refined carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (475 mg/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low (0.5 g/100g) and trans fat is absent, which are favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content from oyster and soy sauce poses a cardiovascular risk; bok choy itself provides beneficial cruciferous phytonutrients and vitamin C, but sodium reduction through sauce modification is strongly recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Bok choy provides sulforaphane and vitamin C (15 mg/100g) with anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is very low and trans fat is absent
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory profile of bok choy; however, high sodium from the sauce may worsen tissue swelling and should be reduced.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bok Choy in Oyster Sauce (Vietnamese: Cải Thìa Sốt Dầu Hào) is a popular Vietnamese side dish consisting of fresh bok choy (cải thìa) that is either blanched in boiling water or briefly stir-fried in a hot wok, then generously coated or drizzled with a savoury, glossy oyster sauce mixture. The sauce typically combines oyster sauce, light soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and a small amount of cornstarch-thickened stock or water to create a rich, umami-forward glaze. The dish delivers a tender-crisp texture with a mild, slightly sweet brassica flavour balanced by the deep savoury saltiness of the oyster sauce. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 60 kcal per 100 g with 2.25 g protein, 3.0 g fat, and 6.0 g carbohydrate. Bok choy is a cruciferous vegetable rich in vitamin C (15 mg/100g), calcium (50 mg/100g), and dietary fibre (1.5 g/100g), contributing anti-inflammatory sulforaphane compounds. The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content (475 mg/100g) from oyster and soy sauce, which makes this dish unsuitable for hypertension or cardiovascular disease patients without sauce modification. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice or noodles in Vietnamese and broader Southeast Asian cuisine contexts.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values were sourced from the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked bok choy (FDC ID 170390) and oyster sauce (FDC ID 172237). Values are plausible and internally consistent. No exact composite dish entry exists in any approved primary source for this specific Vietnamese preparation; values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy component data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: cooked bok choy (FDC ID 170390) and oyster sauce (FDC ID 172237) combined in proportions consistent with standard preparation. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this prepared composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as a single composite entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation not reported in available approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite dish preparation not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or other approved sources; individual component data exists but composite dish values are not published. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible — bok choy and oyster sauce contain no trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (FDC ID 170390 and FDC ID 172237). potassium_mg value of 220 mg/100g is an estimate based on USDA cooked bok choy data (FDC ID 170390 reports approximately 252 mg/100g for plain cooked bok choy; diluted by sauce components). Input record ethnic_tags ('Pho', 'Banh Mi', 'Noodle') were not carried forward as they are not relevant to this dish. country_of_origin in input was listed as 'Singapore' but the dish is Vietnamese in origin; corrected to Vietnam. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed international GI tables (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition) for cooked non-starchy cruciferous vegetables; this is an approved peer-reviewed journal source.
